en: Don't forget that even Auschwitz, for example, is part of the new Reich.
It is part of an area which has been absorbed into Germany.
So as far as they're concerned, this has now got, you know, it's now no longer got the Polish name.
It's now called Auschwitz, which is a German name. It is part of Germany.
And there are German people moving there into this, you know, air comma model town.
And they all know exactly what's going on. Yeah.
You, by the way, have a nice podcast series of four episodes on Auschwitz, the evolution
of the dream world town that becomes a camp, a work camp, then becomes an extermination
camp and a big booner factory for IG Farben, which never produces a single bit of rubber.
So this, for sure, is something I would have to dive deep in.
There's a book you recommended, K.L.?
Yes, it's just called K.L.
It's about the whole concentration camp system, because K is Konzentration in German.
Lager is a camp.
It's an exhaustive book, and I'm full of admiration for him for writing it, just because cheap
as it must have been. I mean, I was very depressed doing that work on Auschwitz, that deep dive.
I just found the whole thing utterly dispiriting. And I've been there a few times, and it's ghastly.
So how he wrote a whole book on it, I don't know.
I think in the details, there's two ways, I think, to look at the Holocaust.
One is man's search for meaning by Viktor Frankl, sort of this philosophical thing about
how a human being can confront that and find meaning, and what does the human condition
look like in the context of such evil?
And then there is the more sort of detailed, okay, well, how do you actually implement
something like the final solution?
So you have this ideology of evil implemented.
And at the fine detail of what are the different technologies used, what are the different
humans and the hierarchy of humans in a camp, how do they, what's the actual experience
of the individual person who shows up at a camp?
Just get in the details, and in those details, I think there's some deep, profound human
truth that can emerge.
The mundane, one step at a time, is how you can achieve evil.
And you can get lost in the mundane. Yes, the banality of evil.
It's incredible.
I think what is so completely horrific is that half the six million were killed by kind
of bullets to the back of the head, and the reason they stopped doing that, and they wanted
to stop doing that, was because the guys, the perpetrators, were finding it so traumatic.
You know, Himmler goes and visits an execution in Ukraine, or maybe he's in the Baltic states,
I can't remember where he goes, but he witnessed some in the summer of 1941.
He thinks, oh, that's horrible, you know, I don't have to do that, I don't want my men having to do that.
I've got to find a more humane way of doing it.
When he's talking about a more humane way of doing it, humane for the executors, executioners,
not for the victims. Because, trust me, Zyklon B is not a nice way to go.
You know, basically, it's bursting all the capillaries in your lungs, it's extremely
painful, and you can no longer breathe.
And it can take up to 20, 25 minutes.
You know, some people, it can take a couple of minutes.
But all of those who are standing naked in that gas chamber, first of all, extremely
humiliated by this process in the first place.
Then there's a sudden realization that they're not having a shower, they're actually being
gassed, and they're all going to die.
Imagine what you're thinking as that process is, you know, because you might be the first,
ko: 그 과정은 알잖아, 네가 첫 번째일 수도 있지만, 첫 번째 사람도 숨을 쉴 수 없고 죽어가고 있어.

en: but you're still going to, even the first person is going to know that I can't breathe and I'm dying.
ko: 그 과정은 알잖아, 네가 첫 번째일 수도 있지만, 첫 번째 사람도 숨을 쉴 수 없고 죽어가고 있어.

en: Everyone else is going to see the first few dying and then going to realize that is what's going to happen to them.
ko: 다른 사람들은 처음 몇 명이 죽어가는 걸 보고, 그게 자신에게도 일어날 거라는 걸 깨닫게 될 거야.

en: And you've got those minutes, sometimes many minutes, where you've got to contemplate that.
ko: 그걸 고민할 수 있는 몇 분이 있어, 때로는 여러 분이.

en: And that's in extreme pain and panic.
ko: 그리고 그건 극심한 고통과 공포 속에서야. 얼마나 잔인한지 생각해봐.

en: And just think about how cruel that is.
ko: 그 과정에서 계속 모욕당하는 거야.

en: While being humiliated all the way through.
ko: 가스실의 인류라는 건 전혀 그런 게 아니야.

en: While being humiliated all the way through.
ko: 가스실의 인류라는 건 전혀 그런 게 아니야.

en: And so, the inverted commas, humanity of the gas chambers is anything but.
ko: 가스실의 인류라는 건 전혀 그런 게 아니야.

en: It's disgusting. And the fact that people could do this is just beyond horrific.
ko: 그리고 유대인 포로들을 데려다가 죽은 사람의 머리카락을 다 잘라내고, 이빨을 뽑게 하고, 그들을 태우는 엘리베이터에 올리는 거야.

en: And then the fact that you are taking your Jewish prisoners and getting them to cut off
ko: 그리고 유대인 포로들을 데려다가 죽은 사람의 머리카락을 다 잘라내고, 이빨을 뽑게 하고, 그들을 태우는 엘리베이터에 올리는 거야.

en: all the hair, pull out the teeth of the dead before you put them on a lift and incinerate
ko: 지금 아우슈비츠에 가면, 독일 사람들이 러시아에 의해 무너지기 전에 파괴한 가스실의 잔해를 볼 수 있어.

en: them. If you go to Auschwitz now and you go to the collapse of blown up gas chambers, which
ko: 여전히 재가 있는 연못도 있고, 뼈 조각들도 남아 있어.

en: the Germans destroyed before the Russians overran them in January 45, you can still see some of the ash ponds.
ko: 그리고 그 기차에서 내릴 때를 상상해봐, 정말 긴 여행 끝에 아무런 편안함도 없이.

en: And there are bits of bone there, but still there from the ash.
ko: 다시 모욕과 고통을 겪어야 했고, 다른 사람들 앞에서 구석에서 변을 봐야 했어.

en: It's just, it is utterly repulsive.
ko: 다시 모욕과 고통을 겪어야 했고, 다른 사람들 앞에서 구석에서 변을 봐야 했어.

en: And imagine arriving from that train on that incredibly long journey where you've had no comforts whatsoever.
ko: 정말 끔찍해. 그리고 도착하자마자 아이들이나 20년을 함께한 남편과 아내가 즉시 분리되는 거야.

en: You've had, again, you've had humiliations and privations, you know, the privations you've
ko: 정말 끔찍해. 그리고 도착하자마자 아이들이나 20년을 함께한 남편과 아내가 즉시 분리되는 거야.

en: had to suffer as a result of that.
ko: 서로 다른 그룹으로 보내져서 가스실로 가는 거지. 그 잔인함의 규모는 정말 엄청나.

en: You know, having to kind of defecate in a bucket in the corner in front of other people.
ko: 서로 다른 그룹으로 보내져서 가스실로 가는 거지. 그 잔인함의 규모는 정말 엄청나.

en: It's just horrendous. And then you get there bewildered and immediately your kids are taken away from you or your,
ko: 이해하기 힘들어. 내가 정말로 화해하기 어려운 건,

en: you know, husband and wife who've been married 20 years, they're separated just like that.
ko: 역사에서의 경고가 중요하다는 거야. 독일은 정말 놀라운 나라야.

en: Sent off into different groups, straight to the gas chambers.
ko: 베토벤과 스트라우스, 괴테의 나라이고, 믿을 수 없는 예술과 문화가 있어.

en: I mean, you know, it is, the scale of cruelty is so immense.
ko: 베토벤과 스트라우스, 괴테의 나라이고, 믿을 수 없는 예술과 문화가 있어.

en: It's hard to fathom.
ko: 역사상 가장 위대한 엔지니어와 과학자들이 살았던 곳이야. 그런데 얼마나 빨리 끔찍한 비인간성으로 뒤바뀌었는지.

en: And the thing that I find really difficult to reconcile, and this is where I think the,
ko: 역사상 가장 위대한 엔지니어와 과학자들이 살았던 곳이야. 그런데 얼마나 빨리 끔찍한 비인간성으로 뒤바뀌었는지.

en: you know, the warning from history is important, is that Germany is such an amazing nation.
ko: 홀로코스트와 가스실, 구덩이에서의 처형으로 나타났어.

en: You know, it's the country of Beethoven and Strauss and of Goethe and incredible art and
ko: 리투아니아나 우크라이나의 작은 장소들에서 말이야. 정말 끔찍해.

en: culture and some of the greatest engineers and scientists have ever lived.
ko: 그리고 이 나라는 불과 10년 전에는 민주국가였어.

en: And look how quickly it flipped into the descent of unspeakable inhumanity, which manifests
ko: 그건 아주 얇은 선이고, 우리는 조심스럽게 걸어야 해.

en: itself in the Holocaust and the gas chambers and the executions into pits and tiny places
ko: 응, 고마워.

en: and creeks in Lithuania or Ukraine or whatever.
I mean, it's just horrendous.
And, you know, this is from a nation which a decade earlier had been a democracy.
It seems like as a human civilization, we walk that Solzhenitsyn line between good and evil.
It's a thin line and we have to walk it carefully.
Yes.
Thank you.
